responseSet.title: A Test of Subliminal Symbiotic Activation as a Means of Alleviating Depression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Test of Subliminal Symbiotic Activation as a Means of Alleviating Depression

Jerry Thornton PHD

Jerry Thornton

Jerry Thornton  

Thirty-six depressive subjects were randomly assigned to one of three treatment groups. Group 1 subjects received 60 subliminal presentations of a slide of two figures and the words MOMMY AND I ARE ONE for male subjects and DADDY AND I ARE ONE for female subjects. Group 2 subjects received 60 subliminal slide presentations of a single figure with the words PEOPLE ARE WALKING. Group 3 subjects did not receive a slide. A pretest and posttest Beck Depression Inventory was administered to assess depression increase or decrease. The “subliminal” therapy was carried out over four different sessions. The data show that Group 1 subjects significantly reduced depression scores from pretest to posttest, whereas the subjects in Groups 2 and 3 did not reduce depression scores. Discussion is centered on comparisons of the data with past findings.

responseSet.title: Helen Block Lewis (1913-1987)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Helen Block Lewis (1913-1987)

Bernard Kalinkowitz PHD

Bernard Kalinkowitz

Bernard Kalinkowitz  

After a brief illness, Helen Block Lewis died in Cambridge, MA, January 18, 1987. That day, Division 39 lost one of its pioneers, a woman who had served as president during 1984-1985; Psychoanalytic Psychology lost not only its founder and guiding spirit but the brilliant editor of all 12 issues published through 1986; psychoanalysis mourned the loss of an outstanding practitioner, a supervisor, a researcher; psychology was deprived of a superior teacher, a scholar; and many of us lost a valued friend whose warmth, humanism, and depth of caring are irreplaceable.

Born August 22, 1913, on Henry Street in Manhattan, Helen grew up in New York City. She had a very close relationship with her father, a physician on the lower East Side. Her father was an old-fashioned neighborhood doctor who knew the detailed histories of the families he tended, and Helen picked up his sense of dedication and concern for the sick and needy as she accompanied him often on his house calls.

Those who knew Helen well were aware of the self-discipline and of the great demands she placed on herself — she was constantly refining her knowledge, never satisfied that she knew enough. An incident from her childhood is paradigmatic, in a sense, of what was to follow. The night before Helen was to go to school for the first time—she must have been close to 6—her father heard her sobbing. He went upstairs to see why she was crying and gently asked what on earth was the matter. “Daddy,” she said, “I'm supposed to start school tomorrow, but how can I go? I don't know anything.”

In 1932, at the age of 19, Helen received a B.A. from Barnard; in 1936, she received an M.A. and a Ph.D. in psychology from Columbia. She was a Phi Beta Kappan and was rewarded with a fellowship for her outstanding scholastic achievements.

She began an illustrious teaching career at Brooklyn College in 1934-2 years before the completion of her doctorate—and remained there until 1942. She next taught courses in social and clinical psychology at the New School for Social Research (1945-1958) and at Swarthmore College.

Seymour Fisher PHD

Seymour Fisher

Seymour Fisher  

In this book, we are presented with still another approach to decoding dreams — the Perceptanalytic Dream Interpretation System. We are told that the usual psychoanalytic mode of dream interpretations is flawed. Piotrowski and Biele contrast their approach with that of Freud's:

More dissimilar conceptions of the role of the manifest dream can hardly be imagined. By reducing the manifest dream's significance to that of a starting point for free, serial associations, classical psychoanalysts assign to manifest dreams a role no more significant than that of day residuals. Dreamers and interpreters contribute to latent content; interpretations are dominated by the desire to identify the wishes that were expected to have been fulfilled in dreams but that were concealed from dreamers' conscious perception by means of disguise. By contrast, perceptanalysts pay careful attention to manifest dreams because these dreams constitute their most valuable data. They do not ask for additional associations, but they preserve the emotional, logical, irrational, and dramatic unity resulting from dream figures' involvement in developing dream plots. (p. 129)

Freud focused on the wish-fulfilling function of the dreams, but Piotrowski and Biele conceptualize dreams as unconscious attempts to clarify the dreamer's “personal intrapsychic conflicts” and to find solutions to the conflicts. They provide detailed “rules” and “axioms” to guide dream interpretation. For example, they state that all traits appearing in a dream and attributed by dreamers to themselves are “assented” (seen as acceptable); whereas traits assigned to dream figures other than the dreamer are “dissented” (experienced as ego alien). [[RunningHead]]

Freud's Discovery of Psychoanalysis

The Politics of Hysteria, William J. McGrath. Ithaca, NY: Cornell University Press, 1986, 336 pp., $25.00.

Paul Roazen PHD

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen  

Freud studies would seem to have entered a new phase. At least from the perspective of one who has been following the subject for the last 25 years, the current scholarly output on the founder of psychoanalysis has several distinctive features. Formerly, books on Freud tended to be written by self-directed individuals driven by a passionate if sometimes idiosyncratic interest in the material. Of course, there has been a steady stream of orthodox psychoanalytic embellishments to the portrait of Freud that Ernest Jones tried to establish. But in addition to such relatively unadventurous and professionally selfserving research, there has been a long line of independent efforts to document and appraise Freud's life.

William McGrath's book is a sign that professional historians have entered the field in strength and with a determination to link Freud's work to its social and cultural surroundings. Professor McGrath is especially interested in the period of the 1890s but explores whatever evidence is available about the intellectual origins of Freud's ideas. The first chapter examines the significance of the Biblical figure of Joseph to Freud; the second looks at the signs of Freud's adolescent rebelliousness; the third quotes from Freud's letters to his friend Eduard Silberstein; the fourth goes over the Fliess period; the fifth discusses the collapse of the seduction theory; the sixth brings up the explicit political underpinnings to early psychoanalysis; and the seventh describes Freud's rivalry with brother figures.

The feminist movement, like any other process, can be examined in the way it effects the realms of science, literature, and the visual arts. It can also be evaluated in its impact on psychoanalysis. The literature from the earliest days of the current feminist movement was antipsychoanalytic and especially anti-Freud. The images and identity of women in classical psychoanalysis did not fit into the zeitgeist of the times. The feminist movement was inspired by the civil rights movement of the 1960s and went through stages of nationalism and segregation. As part of the bond of sisterhood, as well as the growing feeling that men could not understand women's needs, women were warned to stay away from male therapists. Some of the leading writers and practitioners were viewed as adhering too closely to the male model, and women analysts like Deutsch, Salome, and Bonaparte were seen as accommodationists. This occurred as the so-called patriarchal services of psychiatrists waned to be supplanted by clinical psychology, social work, the human potential movement, and social science feminism.

responseSet.title: Imagery as an Aid in Working Through Unconscious Conflicts: A Preliminary Report
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imagery as an Aid in Working Through Unconscious Conflicts

A Preliminary Report

Lloyd Silverman PHD

Lloyd Silverman

Lloyd Silverman   *

A technique innovation designed to aid the working-through process in psychoanalytic treatment is considered. It consists of an analysand, either during or after treatment, imploding psychodynamic themes that have come to be understood as crucially involved in his or her psychology and psychopathology. (“Imploding psychodynamic themes” refers to the person conjuring up affectladen images in which warded-off libidinal and aggressive impulses are expressed and then met with punishment—e.g., abandonment or castration.) Clinical material is cited which suggests that the use of implosive imagery as a psychoanalytic treatment adjunct can lead to the mastery of unconscious conflict, the emergence of new insights, and, occasionally, the retrieval of repressed memories.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Uncontained Projective Identification

The Vicious Circle of Runaway Positive Feedback Loops

Polly Crisp PHD

Polly Crisp

Polly Crisp  

“Uncontained projective identifications can create endless problems in the therapeutic interaction. This article primarily looks at one aspect of this difficulty, namely, that of runaway positive feedback loops. A brief definition of projective identification is given. Containment is then discussed in a more general sense. The manner in which uncontained projective identifications can escalate into positive feedback loops is presented through a specific clinical example. Finally, the mechanism is distinguished from projection and simple behavioral induction.

Structures of Subjectivity

Explorations in Psychoanalytic Phenomenology, George E. Atwood and Robert D. Stolorow. Hillsdale, NJ and London: The Analytic Press, 1984, 132 pp., $19.95.

John Suler PHD

John Suler

John Suler  

Since Kohut's death, a new generation of self psychologists continue to push forward into the areas he pioneered. In Structures of Subjectivity, Atwood and Stolorow offer a valuable new perspective within this psychoanalytic movement. They address the previously neglected need to integrate self psychology with traditional philosophical and psychological theories about the self, including hermeneutics, existential phenomenology, and structuralism. They also present their perspective in the form of a well-organized, comprehensive framework that speaks to the fundamental issues of psychoanalytic psychology — personality, development, psychopathology, and clinical theory and technique.

The foundation for this psychological framework consists of the intriguing concepts of “structures of experience” and “intersubjectivity.” At wood and Stolorow define personality as a system or structure for ordering the cognitive-affective schemata through which a person's experience of self and other acquires meaning. Normal development results in the consolidation of an integrated structure, whereas pathological development leads to insufficient or faulty structuralization in which transitional objects, neurotic symptoms, behavioral enactments, and dreams all serve to avoid selffragmentation through the sensorimotor “concretization” of existing structures. They suggest that intersubjectivity is the interaction between the emerging structures of the child and those of the parental selfobject — an interaction that can result in the healthy mirroring and enhancement of the child's self or in its pathological development by forcing it to accommodate to the faulty psychological structures of the parent. In the intersubjective context of psychoanalytic treatment — through empathy, introspection, and interpretation — the analyst activates and restores the patient's self structure.

responseSet.authorMast: Laura Brent and  Ruth Codier Resch
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Paradigm of Infant-Mother Reciprocity

A Reexamination of “Emotional Refueling”

Laura Brent PhD and Ruth Resch PhD

Ruth Resch

Ruth Resch  

In Mahler, Pine, and Bergman's (1975) concept of “emotional refueling,” an evocative metaphor is used to depict an exchange of energy between mother and infant. This study of videotaped interactive data reexamines the impact of these reunion episodes on the 10-to 12-month-old infant's expression of emotion and attention. Through intentional pursuit of the mother, the baby effectively regulates high and low levels of arousal before reunion to a more organized attentional and affective state after reunion. The four patterns of “intentional reciprocity” are vitalizing reciprocity, soothing reciprocity, mutual attunement, and protective reciprocity. That an infant can use newly emerging cognitive awareness and locomotor capacities to cope with the difficulties of its experience reflects a new level of ego organization with profound implications for the development of internalization, defense, and psychological adaptation.

[[RunningHead]]

Consensus in the Therapeutic Relationship

Beth Carone PHD

Beth Carone

Beth Carone  

This article analyzes and discusses the concept of consensus between patient and therapist in psychoanalytic psychotherapy. This topic has emerged in both psychotherapy research and analytic theory as an important construct and possible curative factor in psychotherapy. These independent lines of investigation will be reviewed and synthesized by considering consensus as a broader construct than simple agreement. Consensus is a creation of the dyad, based on an explicit and mutual effort to define the unique elements of the conscious and unconscious perspectives of patient and therapist. A theoretical understanding of the curative mechanisms of this process is proposed.

Consensus has been discussed extensively and independently in both the the-oretical and research literature on psychotherapy, although often not in direct terms. The term consensus is usually defined as a level of agreement between two people. There is, however, a qualitative aspect of consensus that could be described as mutuality of focus and shared reality. Sullivan (1950) described this aspect of consensus in his explanation of the process of consensual validation: “One' experience in using words has been observed with such care that one has finally learned how to create in the hearer' mind some-thing remotely resembling what one hoped he would think of” (p. 325).

Consensus is not simply agreement, but a creation of the dyad, based on an explicit and mutual defining of the unique elements of the individual perspectives within the dyad. It is the dyadic, or relational, form of consciousness. Recent theoretical and research developments that stress this point are reviewed in this article, and a theoretical understanding of the curative mechanism of this process is proposed. [[RunningHead]]

Identification With a Brain-Damaged Parent

Theoretical Considerations on a Case of Self-Mutilation

David Hartman PHD and Seth Powsner MD

Seth Powsner

Seth Powsner  

A female patient in her 20s with a history of repeated hospitalization for selfmutilation is presented. Countertransference clues prompted reexamination of the patient's symptoms and suggested an identification with an aphasic, hemiparetic parent. The case presented focuses theoretical attention on the interaction between processes of pathological identification and neuropsychological dysfunction. A proposal is made to broaden the concept of identification to include potential variations in neurological, somatosensory, and intellectual function as they may interact with changes in affect. Implications for theory and treatment are explored.

[[RunningHead]]

Empirical Studies of Psychoanalytical Theories

Volume 1, edited by Joseph Masling. Hillsdale, NJ, and London: The Analytic Press, Inc., 1983, 320 pp., $29.95.

George Stricker PHD

George Stricker

George Stricker  

Many psychoanalytic clinicians are comfortable functioning in the clinical world, using experience-near data. Many others are comfortable in the meta psychological world, using more experience-distant concepts. Some are even able to apply theory to practice, and modify one based on their experience of the other. But the bridge between the two, the middle-level described by Mayman, which represents the empirical joining of the theoretical and clinical, operationalized in the form of research which can inform either the clinical or the theoretical, is an area of lessened comfort for many. It is to this middle-level that Masling's book addresses itself, presenting examples of a wide variety of psychoanalytic research projects. Some are studies of psychoanalytic theory and others of psychoanalytic therapy; some are more empirical and others are more theoretical, some are original and others are reviews of already published material; but all provide an empirical grounding for practice. I will review the major findings and contributions of each of the seven chapters, and conclude with some general comments about psychoanalytic research.

responseSet.title: The Concept of God in the Psychological Formation of Females
responseSet.authorMast: Mary Lou Randour and  Julie Bondanza
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of God in the Psychological Formation of Females

Mary Randour PHD and Julie Bondanza PHD

Julie Bondanza

Silver Spring, MD

Julie Bondanza  

We use theory and research to argue that a cultural concept of God profoundly influences female psychological development. God, as the most mythologically and historically significant transitional object, influences the developmental path of an object representation of God, the related self-representation, and the child's future relationship to a larger cultural arena of human living. We use a clinical case example to illustrate how a positive transference and an alternative concept of God mutually influence the elaboration of a God representation derived from a maternal imago, which in turn frees the patient to explore her early object representations. We close by stressing that a culture's myths and symbols, particularly the culture's God concept, have theoretical and clinical relevance in the psychological development of the individual.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Hypnoanalysis

A Professional History and a Challenge

Erika Fromm PHD

Erika Fromm

Erika Fromm  

When I received the Division 39 award last year, I asked Dr. Stockhamer what kind of paper he expected me to present this year. He said, “Talk about your work. Give us a professional autobiography.” So that is what I did.

I grew up in a very orthodox and authoritarian German Jewish family and became a rebel against orthodoxy at the age of 15. At that age, for the first time I became aware of Freud's work. I literally “dis-covered” Freud's work at age 15: I was asked to dust every book in my parents' library, and hidden behind a row of other books I found a number of Freud's writings which my father, a physician, clearly did not want his children to see. That, of course, aroused my curiosity. Secretly, I read all I could find, and remained fascinated by psychoanalysis. I also have remained a rebel against orthodoxy ever since, fighting orthodoxy—even in psychoanalysis. I am deeply convinced that orthodoxy and conservatism lead to stagnation in science as well as in religion. I am proud to have pioneered in various ways in my profession, and I am happy to share my experiences with those of you who also are pioneers. And, I hope to encourage those with an adventurous spirit to go out and extend the frontiers of our profession. Pioneering is often lonesome and painful; but it is also deeply satisfying.

I am a Nazi refugee. The Nazis started to gain political influence when I was in high school, and had come to full power by the time I was 22 years old. My dissertation—done under Max Wertheimer, the father of Gestalt theory—was nearly finished, and I still was able to finish it during that year and get my Ph.D. from the University of Frankfurt. Four weeks later I fled to Holland, where I got a research assistantship in the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Amsterdam.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lloyd Silverman (1929-1986)

Morris Eagle PHD and David Wolitzky PHD

David Wolitzky

David Wolitzky  

Lloyd Silverman was born on December 20, 1929 and was raised in the Bronx. He went to the local elementary school and then to De Witt Clinton High School. He graduated with a BA from New York University (1951) where he majored in psychology. In 1952, Lloyd enrolled in the clinical doctoral psychology program at New York University. He did his Ph.D. dissertation on the validity of evaluations made from projective techniques and received his degree in 1958. During his graduate training, Lloyd interned in various Veterans Administration hospitals and worked with Dr. Fred Brown at Mt. Sinai Hospital in New York.

Lloyd worked for many years as a Research Psychologist for the Veterans Administration and was also a part-time faculty member at New York University where he taught a number of graduate courses and supervised many Ph.D. dissertations. He also carried on a part-time psychoanalytically oriented private practice and was on the faculty of the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research and the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. Recently, he joined the graduate faculty at Long Island University.

In 1958, Lloyd and Doris (nee Klein) married and had four children: Ilena, Mara, Gillian, and Stefan.

These are some bare facts that tell very little about Lloyd. Throughout his adult and professional life, Lloyd was always a highly productive researcher and theorist. But this statement too captures little of Lloyd's qualities. He was devoted to psychoanalysis, and his life's work was dedicated to taking psychoanalytic ideas seriously and testing them out empirically. For Lloyd, ideas were not merely things to think about and play with; they were to be approached with utter seriousness, pushed as far as possible, and used for meaningful pragmatic purposes (e.g., to effect therapeutic change). It was impossible to know Lloyd and not experience his intensity and seriousness of purpose and his dedication to productive, empirical inquiry as a means of resolving long-standing, controversial psychoanalytic propositions.

responseSet.title: Development of the Concept of Anatomical Constancy: Part 1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Development of the Concept of Anatomical Constancy

Part 1

Ken Feiner Psy. D.

Ken Feiner

Ken Feiner  

Three developmental levels in the acquisition of anatomical constancy were identified in a group of children between 2 and 5 years of age on the basis of (a) a reproducible Guttman scale (the Anatomical Constancy Scale) of answers to sets of questions and (b) performance on specific tasks. The three levels that represent operationalized dimensions of the concept of anatomical constancy entail (a) the integration of part images of the body into a whole body image, (b) the establishment of a cohesive and inviolable body image, and (c) the capacity to retain a sense of the body's continuity over time. Transitions from one level to the next reflect advances in the formation of psychic structure.

The results of this study indicate that the body image undergoes systematic changes as it develops into a constant, cohesive and inviolable whole. As children get older or acquire specific cognitive capacities, their body images become increasingly stable, cohesive, and continuous.

Recent years have witnessed an outpouring of psychoanalytic and cognitive development literature on the evolution of a cohesive body image and sense of self. Cognitive developmental theorists have suggested that the development of a constant and cohesive body image is sequential in nature and occurs as a function of cognitive development. Specifically, Kohlberg (1966) claimed that it is the presence of concepts of conservation, an achievement of the concrete operational stage, that enables the child to recognize those aspects of the body that remain invariant and continuous in spite of alterations in its configuration (e.g., size).

Editor's Note: This Brief Report is Part 1 of two parts. Part 2 will appear in Volume 5, Number 1.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy, Clinical Evidence, and the Self-Fulfilling Prophecy

Joseph Masling PHD and Ira Cohen PHD

Ira Cohen

Ira Cohen  

Two paradoxes of psychotherapy research — (a) various therapeutic approaches produce approximately equal outcomes despite wide differences in orientations, and (b) all psychotherapies generate clinical evidence that supports their theoretical positions—can be understood as instances of therapists systematically rewarding and extinguishing various client behaviors. Therapists’ belief in their theories serves as a self-fulfilling prophecy, both to produce change in clients and to confirm the theory. Evidence supplied by the client is, in part, a product of the interaction with a therapist. It is as mistaken to accept clinical evidence uncritically as it is to reject it out of hand. Experimental confirmation of clinical evidence is crucial.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Devalued (Unloved, Repugnant) Self — A Second Facet of Narcissistic Vulnerability in the Aggressive, Conduct-Disordered Child

Brent Willock PHD

Brent Willock

Brent Willock  

The concept of the devalued self complements a previous presentation concerning another fundamental facet of narcissistic vulnerability that I termed the disregarded self. Whereas the disregarded self reflects the child's fear that others simply do not care about him, the devalued self reflects his belief that there is something grossly repugnant about him that makes people reject and abandon him in disgust. Much of the conduct-disordered child's antisocial behavior and character stance can be understood as an attempt to cope with and defend against the hurt, the anger, and the anxiety associated with these aspects of selfstructure, these feelings of being unloved and unlovable. Manifestations of, and defenses against, the devalued self are discussed, primarily in terms of how they can be observed during the course of intensive treatment. Implications of the concept for individual psychotherapy and milieu treatment are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Fate of the Narcissistic Personality

Lost in Time

Karen Maroda PHD

Karen Maroda

Milwaukee, WI

Karen Maroda  

The major themes presented in this article deal with the self-conceptualization of narcissistic personality disorders based on the experience of time. The “lost in time” hypothesis is descriptive, based on psychotherapies with narcissistically disturbed adults. The basic premise is that these individuals tend to present with a history of both immaturity and inadequate development and with a tendency toward precocity and demonstration of unusual emotional sensitivity or other talent. The forward and backward shifting of these patients' lives is expressed through various successes and failures, as well as through a feeling of being perpetually “in transition” and fearing stagnation and mediocrity. Clinical implications for this “syndrome” are discussed in detail, with special emphasis on difficulties of the patient in making a commitment to treatment and being able to function in the present, demonstrating a preference for the past and/or future.

Very early in my life it was too late. It was already too late when I was eighteen.

—Marguerite Duras (1984/1985, p. 4)

This quote from Duras's autobiographical novel reflects the fatalism of a young woman robbed of her childhood by emotional deprivation and economic necessity. Priding herself on her ability to mimic the chicness of grown women, she dressed as an adult while still a little girl. At age 15, she became the lover of a foreign merchant; by age 18, she was poignantly aware of her core of emptiness and despair and knew it was “too late.” But too late for what?

responseSet.title: A Reconceptualization of Adaptive Regression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Reconceptualization of Adaptive Regression

John Rosegrant PHD

John Rosegrant

John Rosegrant  

The concept of adaptive regression is useful because it emphasizes that regression can be adaptive, but is misleading in suggesting that adaptive regressions are a discrete and unitary set of phenomena. Recently, writers have stated that primary process and creativity are not regressive, but these ideas imply an implausibly sharp break between regressive and adaptive phenomena, and are contradicted by empirical observations. Regression may be adaptive in a variety of ways: It may be chosen and used for communication or for privately adaptive ends, or it may occur unbidden, but nevertheless, be used for communication or privately adaptive ends. A more meaningful question than “Is regression adaptive or maladaptive,” is “How is regression adaptive, maladaptive, or both?”

[[RunningHead]]

Recovery and Verification of Memories of Childhood Sexual Trauma

Judith Herman MD and Emily Schatzow MED

Emily Schatzow

Emily Schatzow  

Fifty-three women outpatients participated in short-term therapy groups for incest survivors. This treatment modality proved to be a powerful stimulus for recovery of previously repressed traumatic memories. A relationship was observed between the age of onset, duration, and degree of violence of the abuse and the extent to which memory of the abuse had been repressed. Three out of four patients were able to validate their memories by obtaining corroborating evidence from other sources. The therapeutic function of recovering and validating traumatic memories is explored.

[[RunningHead]]

Orality as a Factor in Interpersonal Yielding

Robert Bornstein PhD , Joseph Masling PhD and Frederick Poynton

Frederick Poynton

Frederick Poynton  

The results of two separate studies are reported. In the first, 30 undergraduate male subjects, designated high-oral on the basis of the number of oral and dependent Rorschach responses provided, were individually paired with 30 loworal subjects, and were asked to reach a consensus regarding a neutral topic on which they had previously disagreed. In contrast to earlier studies on orality and group conformity, the low-oral subjects deferred to the judgment of the high-oral subjects in 20 out of 30 dyads, χ2(1, N = 30) = 3.33, p < .10. A replication of the original study on a new sample of 20 high-oral and 20 low-oral subjects, confirmed our original findings (binomial test, p = .02).

[[RunningHead]]

Behind the “Seens”

The Inner Experience of at Least One Psychoanalyst

Sabert Basescu PHD

Sabert Basescu

Sabert Basescu  

Clinical psychoanalysis has increasingly concerned itself with the contributions made to the psychoanalytic relationship by the therapist's personality, transferences, and countertransferences. In keeping with that concern, this article focuses on my own thoughts and feelings as I've observed them in the course of my functioning as a psychoanalyst. Questions like: What's the ongoing nature of my experience in sessions?; How come I say what I do say?; What prompts the interventions I make?; have guided my observations. My intention is to contribute to our understanding of what really goes on in analytic sessions, not simply what is formally portrayed.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy With Panic States: A Case Presentation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy With Panic States

A Case Presentation

James Stoeri PHD

James Stoeri

James Stoeri  

Recent research into the etiology, diagnosis, and treatment of panic states has emphasized (a) their biological basis, (b) the validity of the DSM-III diagnosis of “Panic Disorder,” and (c) behavior therapy and/or medication as the treatment modalities of choice. According to this literature, psychoanalytic psychotherapy with panic states is specifically contraindicated. This article suggests that the proliferation of action-oriented therapies with panic states can be viewed as, at least in part, a response to the atmosphere created by these patients of an urgent need for action that would bring relief. It goes on to focus, via a case presentation, on the unique contribution of the psychoanalytic approach to the understanding and treatment of panic states. Central to the case is the therapist's gradual insight into the countertransference response to the “atmosphere of urgency,” which enables both patient and therapist to tolerate better the anxieties attending the pursuit of a psychoanalytic inquiry.

responseSet.title: Parallel Process as Transference-Countertransference Interaction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Parallel Process as Transference-Countertransference Interaction

Alan Grey PHD and John Fiscalini PHD

John Fiscalini

John Fiscalini  

This article presents a new view of parallel process. Previous explanations are criticized as being too narrow or as employing excessively complex and operationally untestable conceptualizations, including reifications of metaphors. Our observations indicate that parallel process is a chain reaction that may occur in any interconnected series of interpersonal situations that are structurally and dynamically similiar in significant respects. It is not limited to the supervisory situation. Typically it involves intertwined concerns about authority and dependency, and the participants need to conceal their attempted solutions to those concerns, leading to an interlocking series of parallel transference-countertransference integrations. The best understanding of these processes is achieved when the motivations of all the participants are examined and over-laps in their character structures are identified.

responseSet.title: What Are Little Girls Made Of?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Are Little Girls Made Of?

Doris Silverman PHD

Doris Silverman

Doris Silverman  

Research is presented that supports the thesis of the special significance of bonding for females. Although investigators of neonatal behavior have written of the importance of bonding for both male and female infants, there are data suggesting that its salience is more compelling for female infants. Neonatal bonding is more immediate and sustaining and allows for interactive experiences earlier and more frequently in females than males. The research findings cited are considered in relation to Freud's developmental views of women and his psychoanalytic writings on penis envy, the Oedipus complex, and object relations. The concept of autonomy in psychoanalysis is also considered from the vantage point of female development. It is argued, in this context, that the powerful bonding proclivities of females often have been viewed as a developmental failure that serves as a constraint on psychoanalytic theorizing about females and that can handicap women in psychoanalytic treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychodynamics of Obesity

A Review

Joyce Slochower PhD

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower  

The psychoanalytic model of obesity continues to be controversial among research-oriented theorists. Although empirical tests of the psychoanalytic model initially yielded many failures to support the role of affect in overeating, recent experimental data confirm the centrality of this dynamic. This article presents evidence supporting the psychodynamic model of obesity and discusses some developmental implications for these data.

responseSet.authorMast: Robert D. Stolorow and  Bernard Brandchaft
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developmental Failure and Psychic Conflict

Robert Stolorow PHD and Bernard Brandchaft MD

Bernard Brandchaft

Bernard Brandchaft  

A focus on affect integration and its derailments illuminates the complex interrelationships between developmental failure and the formation of psychic conflict, both in childhood and in the psychoanalytic situation. The specific contexts in which conflict takes form are situations in which central affect states of the child cannot be integrated because they fail to evoke the requisite responsiveness from the caregiving surround. Such unintegrated feeling states become the source of enduring inner conflict, because they are experienced as threats to both the person's established psychological organization and to the maintenance of vitally needed ties. In treatment, the emergence of unintegrated affect is resisted for fear that the analyst will replicate the faulty responsiveness of the childhood milieu.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fixation and the Road Not Taken

Peter Shabad PhD

Peter Shabad

Peter Shabad  

In this article, the psychic and existential impact that fixation has on an individual's life is addressed. A person may drift through years that are dictated by the circuitous path of a fixation and its vicissitude of the repetition compulsion. When such “wasted” time is viewed through the revelations gained later in psychotherapy, insight becomes a double-edged sword. The regret of not having used one's life opportunely is superimposed on the analyses of transference and infantile conflicts, thus prolonging treatment. The phenomena of loss and incomplete mourning are used to illustrate the path taken from fixation through psychic drifting to regret. The therapeutic necessity of working through such regret is emphasized.

Is the “Impossible Profession” Possible?

Stephen Appelbaum PhD

Stephen Appelbaum

Stephen Appelbaum  

To form a psychoanalytic identity, one should consider the many versions of psychoanalytic theory and technique and learn to be comfortable applying whatever one espouses, despite the recognition that many issues of theory, technique, selection, and training are unsettled.
